item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This scip had me cry-laughing. The accidental discovery of a widespread yet taboo incest fetish among sentient plants was so much more glorious than a dry systematic experiment log would have been.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: absolutely incredible. Funny, witty, and incredibly interesting. I already +1'd before it even got into the funny bit on how interesting they were in the first place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm pretty sure those disciplinary logs don't actually exist but it'd be hilarious to read them.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I can't put into words how much I love the idea that these intelligent plants that have such large superiority complexes they'd refuse to communicate with another sentient species for two years. Yet they give in when they get some plant porn.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This one went from legitimately just an interesting and cool skip to being one of the funniest I've read on here, thanks to that transcript with Mr. Jackson.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Filthy, unconscious animal!

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I thought this article was fantastic all the way through. I am sad that I haven't seen this before now. +1, and I would upvote it twice if I could.

My reactions as I read through the article:

SCP-3171-053: That is literally the dumbest thing I’ve ever heard.

We have discovered intelligent life, and it's a bunch of literary snobs.

  1. Exceptions to the previous item occur exceedingly rarely, and only by instances located at the far edges of the savanna disconnected from the main component of the root structure.

So, plant hillbillies.

It is conjectured that the act of sharing sustenance with family members has the nature of a sexual taboo among SCP-3171. While all instances of SCP-3171 express disgust at the notion publicly (as when Mr. Williams mentioned sharing a bowl of ice cream with his mother), in private many of them will request to hear detailed accounts of such “incestuous” encounters by Foundation personnel.

So, these intelligent plants have an incest taboo. And an incest fetish. (I think I already figured this a paragraph or two back, but this spelled it out.)

Alice: out of all the morally questionable stuff we do here, this one isn’t in the top hundred that keep me up at night. Denied. O5-██

As others have said, this is brilliant. It's funny because it's true.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First we boiled brains and created monsters that are barely vulnerable to fire, then we started feeding giant eels that cause you to forget and blur everyone's memories together…

…and now we're talking about basic interactions and indirectly jerking off plants. 10/10.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wonder what Mr. Jackson thinks about him being technically a sex worker now, the whole realization is hilarious. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I almost puked. 10/10 would recommend.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Needed a good read, and this delivered. I hope that sharing an upvote isn't sexual, but wouldn't mind getting my mitts on some amnestics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is there anything to be done about the black-boxed section in the second footnote shooting off the edge of the page? I assume the answer would be to add spaces between words, and would be at the author's discretion, if they had actual text in mind when determining the length of the section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: vore is cannibalism. It is explicitly stated in the article that the fluid thing is roughly analogous to incest, no need to try and cram a 3rd fetish in there.

item.title: What brought it home for me
item.content:encoded:
Out of everything in this skip, it was this line that was both the funniest and the most "real" to me:

Alice: out of all the morally questionable stuff we do here, this one isn’t in the top hundred that keep me up at night. Denied. O5-██

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mr. Jackson- Hey, SCP-3171-297. We just discovered some serious tale, for just ten sacs, we will tell you a tale of a beer festival.

Where i can vote for collaboration communication log?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Nope, but they are currently hosted on imgur (and are also sizable), and if that site hiccups, so will the images.

I'll localize the images right now.

ETA: aaaand done. Should also load faster too, since I shrunk the images down a bit.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Nope. Maybe try clearing your cookies and refreshing?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Are the images broken for anyone else?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Throughout the article I kept expecting the Foundation to conclude that the usefulness of amnestics outweighed the morality of genocide against a sapient, poetry-spouting plant species. And then it turned into a Foundation-mandated sex hotline.

This is alien lifeforms done well. Not just for biological differences but for completely different social paradigms, hypocrisy, racism and primal similarities.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh my christ, this is like the human equivalent of… bestiality incest vore. (dunno what sipping fluids with another person is to these plants, but similarity to the act of eating seems like…vore?)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This skip was overall pretty funny, I liked it.

One super minor nitpick - the research team probably would have used a recurrent neural network (which is used for time-dependent data such as audio and language) rather than a convolutional neural network (which is primarily used for image data).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: the first time i saw this, i made the horrible, horrible mistake of skipping over it. now that i've finally sat down and read this thing, it's definitely become a contender for my Top 10 Favorite Scips. i'd give this a +2 if i could.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Daheck with the title?" -Me before reading
"[Insert my laughing]" -Me while reading and after reading
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3171-241: Animal, say something valuable.

SCP-3171-033: Animal, tell us a poem.

When I first saw this, it became my new favorite thing ever.

THEN I read the rest of it. And am now deliriously in love with these stuck-up plants and their filthy, filthy minds.

item.title: Upvoted
item.content:encoded:
"Alice: out of all the morally questionable stuff we do here, this one isn’t in the top hundred that keep me up at night. Denied. O5-██"

I love this so much. It's like "Listen, most of the amnestics we get by feeding a giant sea serpent who consumes conscious thought. Honestly, the plant incest is an improvement."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Naughty plants
+1

item.title: Christ
item.content:encoded: Can we go back to feeding innocents to the Eal, please?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
every single day the magic word is 'butterflies'.

Yes, but the pronunciation and syllable stress is different for each day of the week.

Also sometimes the magic word is "betta".

incidentally, today I did some repotting. brb putting googly eyes on all my plants

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Headcanoned; 'cept in Zyn's Playhouse, every single day the magic word is 'butterflies'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Felt like I needed to turn my screen away from the plant in my room for this. I can still feel it judging me silently.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt gross reading this.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really wouldn't be surprised if Zyn's house was like Peewee's Playhouse, where everything has googly eyes and talks.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: When I hit the Jackson transcript, I started laughing and saying, "What the fuck?!" for fifteen seconds. Take your upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A perverted, vulvate-looking plant that gets off to incest?
Oh god my sides.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I mean, they're supposed to be embarrassed about the particular topic, so I don't think they would announce it through public channel.

But hey, it's funny.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-3171-067: [TRANSLATION ERROR]1

Footnotes
1. Roughly: "That's my fetish!"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was killed by the skip's critique of human poetry, and it got better from there. Wait until they hear about punch bowls. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I laughed at the second mention of a disciplinary log (it was basically a callback by that point,) as well as the O5's response to Alice. Good stuff.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I didn't know what to expect from this skip, but it nonetheless surprised me with how funny it was. Well done! +1

I do want to note for you that the 800 pounds figure ought to be 360 kilograms, though.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Of all the silly skips that I have enthusiastically upvoted, this doesn't make the top 100 that I feel vaguely guilty about :)

As a homage to the tone of 1171, I'd say that this is an unqualified success.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Incest loving plants… Welcome to the Foundation

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It somehow manages to be silly while still being an interesting take on inhuman intelligence. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Alice: out of all the morally questionable stuff we do here, this one isn’t in the top hundred that keep me up at night. Denied. O5-██.

Absolutely incredible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So now you made me tag an article with both 'plant' and 'sexual'. Why are you doing this to me. And why are you making me love and upvote an article about incest-loving plants? God damn it.

.. So when do we start producing Plant-GoT for these guys?

item.title: OH GOD WHY
item.content:encoded:
AAAAAAAH!

releases +1's

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Saw this during one of Zyn's draft raid, loved it there, love it here. Hearty +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In my head, all I can think is that these plants are all very, very bad at poetry but all fake appreciation. This someone makes this article infinitely funnier to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: (Originally wrote something totally misunderstanding your joke here — just realized I can't delete my comments on this site).

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: you're telling me your plants have eyes

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I was sold on an upvote several times throughout; there's just too much to love here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Glad you enjoyed it, and thanks for the feedback — I fixed the placement of the first image.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I have almost two dozen small houseplants in my room and I feel the need to simultaneously read this out loud to them and cover their eyes while I read it.

The interviews are by far the best part. Upvoted!

as a note though, is there a reason that the first image is placed so low in the page? Usually in standard formatting the code for the first image follows right after the code for the rating module

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Who devised this?! Who the heck made this?! How could you possibly make an article that fully justify—nay, reliant on unprofessionalism and make it work?! Here, I would give you a hug or a kiss on the cheek, but since it's physically impossible through internet take this upvote instead.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thanks :)

The first draft I played around with had a lot more details on the language issue, roughly along the following lines:

— The plants can detect events in the real world through sound + the ability to detect uneven light distributions on their stem, which effectively lets them see.

— Correlating events occurring in the real world to their chirps allowed the research team to construct a primitive dictionary (e.g., containing words like sun, rain, etc) as well as understand the underlying grammar of the language.

— Remaining words (for example, dumb vs smart) are worked out from context (i.e., a plant whose poem is called dumb is more hesistant to offer another one).

— All this is done in 9 months (as opposed to 50 years) with the aid of some futuristic machine learning software, which the foundation has in the late 90s.

Ultimately, I ended up not using all this material — it added some verisimilitude but bogged down the entry in detail.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is hilarious! The clinical tone breaks and unprofessional behavior would sink most articles for me, but this piece embraces the silly attitude with gusto and is better off for it. Nicely done.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It's an incredibly silly piece, and the concept of would-be-alien entities speaking English is incredibly old hat… but the little details, like using a convolutional neural network to translate the plant language, and the sheer chutzpah of writing about incest-loving plants garners an upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: You are right about timeline — major misstep on my part. I'll push it forward a decade.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The transcript puts this one over the top for me. One thing that did bother me though - the timeline is just plain off. First contact was by the Foundation in 1989 yet these cacti came to their attention after an .mp3 was posted to a university website. Neither the world wide web nor the .mp3 format existed prior to 1991 or so (the latter, 1993). If you pushed it forward a decade it'd be more plausible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
— So I made neither of the images but both are licensed under creative commons:

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:Cephalocereus_columna-trajani_1.jpg

https://commons.wikimedia.org/wiki/File:HydnoraAfricanaKarasburgDistrictNamibia2002Musselman.jpg

— Thanks to Wogglebug for giving me feedback (I posted a couple of times in the drafts forum but only one person ended up giving me feedback).

— The SCP designation is an attempt on my part to pay a tribute to SCP-1171, which had the blend of the serious and the absurd I was aiming for.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mr. Williams: The gunk they call ice cream here is some kind of wet slop.

"It's right off the truck and it's—it's //soft!//"
